name: LivePerson Inc
description: Pioneer in enterprise conversational AI and chatbot technology, providing the Conversational Cloud platform that enables businesses to automate customer interactions via messaging, voice, and AI-powered bots. Founded in 1995 and once the dominant enterprise chatbot vendor. Revenue peaked at ~$514M in 2022. Executed a 1:15 reverse stock split in Oct 2025.
disruption: Perhaps the most ironic AI casualty: an AI company destroyed by better AI. Before ChatGPT, LivePerson was the go-to enterprise chatbot platform. After ChatGPT launched, generative AI models from OpenAI, Anthropic, and others commoditized conversational AI—enterprises could now build superior chatbots using foundation models directly, reducing the need for LivePerson's proprietary platform. Revenue has roughly halved from ~$514M (2022) to a projected ~$240-255M (2025), declining 19-26% YoY across recent quarters. Net retention rate dropped to ~80%, reflecting persistent customer cancellations and downsells. Stock has fallen ~98% on a split-adjusted basis from Nov 29, 2022 (~$10.68 pre-split equivalent) to ~$3.04. The company is attempting to pivot by integrating LLMs into its platform (Bella AI), but faces intense competition from AI-native alternatives and carries a heavy debt load.

name: Chegg Inc
description: Online education platform offering textbook rentals, homework help, tutoring, and study tools to college and high school students. Peaked at ~$14.7B market cap during COVID remote-learning boom.
disruption: The most direct AI casualty in public markets. ChatGPT and other free AI tools have replaced Chegg's core homework help product, causing subscriber losses of 30%+ YoY. Stock has fallen 99% from its 2021 peak. Non-subscriber traffic dropped 49% after Google AI Overviews launched. Company has cut 45% of workforce and sued Google over AI search summaries.

name: TTEC Holdings Inc
description: Global customer experience (CX) outsourcing firm operating call centers and contact centers across 22 countries with ~50,000 employees. Provides outsourced customer care, technical support, and digital CX consulting.
disruption: AI chatbots and virtual agents are directly replacing the human contact center agents that form TTEC's core business model. Stock has fallen ~95% from its pandemic high. Clients are hesitant to commit spending due to AI uncertainty, and the per-seat billing model is structurally threatened by AI automation. Revenue declining ~5% annually with high debt load (5.5x market cap).

name: Endava plc
description: London-headquartered technology services company providing outsourced software development, digital transformation, and IT consulting. Operates nearshore delivery centers across Eastern Europe (Romania, Moldova, Bulgaria) and Latin America, serving clients in financial services, payments, healthcare, and retail. ~11,000 employees.
disruption: AI coding tools (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude) directly reduce the volume of outsourced software development work that is Endava's core business. Clients can accomplish more with fewer external developers, shrinking the billable hours and headcount-based engagements that drive revenue. Stock has fallen ~93% from its Nov 2022 level (~$71 to ~$5). Revenue declined 5-9% YoY through FY2026, and the company swung to a loss as it invested heavily in an AI pivot (Dava.Flow methodology) that has yet to offset the erosion of traditional time-and-materials contracts. Management explicitly warns of near-term revenue headwinds from AI-driven automation compressing legacy delivery models.

name: Expensify Inc
description: Cloud-based expense management platform for receipt scanning, expense reporting, corporate card management, and travel booking. Primarily serves SMBs. Went public in 2021 at a ~$4B valuation. ~626,000 paid members as of Jan 2026, down from ~684,000 a year prior.
disruption: AI-powered automation from larger competitors (SAP Concur, Brex, Ramp) is eroding Expensify's core value proposition of simplifying expense workflows—tasks that AI now handles natively inside broader finance platforms. Stock fell ~90% from $9.17 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$0.95. Revenue declined 7.6% in FY2024 to $139M and continued falling through 2025. Paid members dropped 6% YoY. The company swung to a $21.4M net loss in FY2025, and 2026 free cash flow guidance was slashed 66%. While Expensify is investing in its own AI agent (Concierge), the competitive moat has narrowed as AI-native fintech tools make standalone expense management less differentiated.

name: Sprout Social Inc
description: Cloud-based social media management platform providing publishing, scheduling, analytics, social listening, engagement, and influencer marketing tools for businesses and agencies. Priced at a premium ($249+/month per seat) targeting mid-market and enterprise customers. ~30,000 customers with ~3,800 contributing $30K+ ARR. Revenue of ~$406M in FY2025.
disruption: AI is compressing the value of specialized social media management software from multiple angles. ChatGPT and AI writing tools can generate social posts, captions, and marketing copy that SMBs previously needed Sprout's content tools to create. Cheaper AI-powered competitors (SocialPilot at $30/mo, Buffer at $6/mo) now bundle AI content generation, sentiment analysis, and scheduling at a fraction of Sprout's $249/seat price, driving SMB churn. Dollar-based net retention rate collapsed to 100% in FY2025—meaning expansion revenue was entirely wiped out by downsells and cancellations. Revenue growth decelerated from 31% (2023) to 22% (2024) to 12.7% (2025) with just 7.7% projected for 2026. Stock has fallen 89% from $55.16 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$5.79, and is down 95% from its 2021 peak. The broader SaaS sector rout—driven by fears that AI commoditizes horizontal software—has hit Sprout especially hard because its core functions (content creation, scheduling, analytics, community management) are exactly the tasks generative AI handles well. The company launched 'Sprout AI' and an agentic AI analyst called 'Trellis' in late 2025, but these features haven't stemmed the valuation collapse as investors question whether a $249/seat social media tool can survive when AI makes the underlying tasks near-free.

name: Definitive Healthcare Corp
description: Provider of healthcare commercial intelligence data and analytics, offering a SaaS platform with detailed profiles on hospitals, physicians, insurance claims patterns, and clinical activity across the US healthcare system. Serves pharma/life sciences companies, health systems, and healthcare IT vendors who use the data for commercial strategy, sales targeting, and market analysis. Revenue peaked at ~$251M in 2023. ~678 employees. Market cap ~$120M, down from ~$2.4B at IPO peak.
disruption: AI is commoditizing the curated healthcare data intelligence that Definitive Healthcare packages and sells at premium subscription prices. AI tools can now synthesize claims data, map physician referral networks, analyze prescribing patterns, and generate commercial insights for pharma sales teams—core use cases for DH's platform. AI-native competitors like Komodo Health are using modern AI architectures and purpose-built healthcare AI engines (Komodo's 'Marmot') to generate patient-level insights that bypass traditional claims data aggregation, directly threatening DH's data moat. Stock has fallen 89% from $10.69 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$1.15. Revenue has declined for two consecutive years—from $251M (2023) to $242M (2024) to $241M (2025)—with 2026 guidance projecting a further 6-9% decline to $220-226M. Net dollar retention has weakened as upsell motions face pressure, and remaining performance obligations dropped 18% YoY as customers shift to shorter one-year deals. Stephens cut its price target from $3 to $2 citing AI-related uncertainty. While management positions AI as a 'tailwind' and plans GenAI features for its Vue platform, the structural reality is that AI makes healthcare data synthesis dramatically cheaper and more accessible outside DH's walled-garden platform—the same dynamic eroding Clarivate and other premium data aggregators.

name: Getty Images Holdings Inc
description: Global visual content marketplace providing stock photos, editorial images, videos, and music to businesses and media. Library of 700M+ files. Announced $3.7B merger with Shutterstock in Jan 2025.
disruption: AI image generators (Midjourney, DALL-E, Stable Diffusion, Adobe Firefly) directly displace demand for generic stock photography. Getty's Creative segment declined ~5% YoY in 2024. Billions of AI images generated monthly now compete with stock libraries. Getty has tried to pivot by licensing its data to AI companies and building its own AI generator, but the core business of selling individual photo licenses is eroding.

name: Ziprecruiter Inc
description: Online employment marketplace connecting job seekers with employers through AI-powered matching technology. Two-sided platform with 4M+ businesses and 180M+ job seekers. Revenue peaked at $905M in 2022.
disruption: AI threatens both sides of the recruiting marketplace: it can automate candidate screening, resume matching, and initial outreach that employers currently pay platforms to do. Revenue has declined from $905M (2022) to $449M (2025). While macro hiring slowdown is the primary driver, AI-driven hiring tools are compressing the value of job board intermediaries and reducing the number of human roles companies need to fill.

name: Bumble Inc
description: Women-first online dating platform operating Bumble (where women make the first move), Badoo (global dating app popular in Europe/Latin America), and Bumble For Friends. ~50 million total users across 150+ countries, though paying users fell 20.5% to 3.3 million by Q4 2025. IPO'd in 2021 at a ~$8.6B valuation. Full-year 2025 revenue of $965.7M, down 9.9% YoY. Absorbed $1.039B in non-cash impairment charges in 2025. Market cap ~$450M, down from ~$14B at its 2021 peak.
disruption: AI is accelerating a multi-front crisis for dating platforms. AI-generated fake profiles now flood dating apps—user reports suggest up to 80% of profiles on major platforms may be fake or AI-generated, up from 10-15% before the 2023 AI boom. Norton blocked 17 million dating scam attacks in Q4 2025 alone (+19% YoY), with AI enabling scammers to generate convincing photos, craft messages that fool 70% of recipients, and operate at unprecedented scale. This erodes the trust and authenticity that dating apps depend on. Simultaneously, AI companion apps (Replika, Character.ai) are competing for the emotional connection users seek, with 25% of young adults believing AI could replace real romantic relationships. 'Swipe fatigue' has intensified as users can no longer distinguish real people from bots. Stock has fallen 84% from $22.40 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$3.60. Paying users declined 11.5% to 3.7M in 2025, with 79% of Gen Z users dropping regular use of dating platforms. Bumble is attempting an AI-powered pivot with 'Bee,' an AI dating concierge that curates matches based on psychology rather than photos, but the company faces a paradox: AI is both the disease destroying user trust and the medicine it's betting on to survive.

name: Trueblue Inc
description: Staffing and workforce solutions provider offering temporary labor, on-demand staffing, recruitment process outsourcing, and managed services across construction, manufacturing, and transportation. Operates PeopleReady, PeopleScout, and PeopleManagement brands.
disruption: AI and automation threaten the temporary staffing model from two directions: AI-powered hiring platforms reduce need for staffing intermediaries, and automation/robotics reduce demand for the blue-collar temp labor that is TrueBlue's core business. Revenue declined 8% YoY in Q1 2025 and 22% in Q4 2024. The company faces structural headwinds as warehouse automation, self-checkout, and AI-driven logistics reduce demand for temporary workers.

name: Forrester Research Inc
description: Global research and advisory firm providing proprietary analyst reports, data, consulting, and events to technology and business leaders. Sells premium access to research insights, vendor evaluations (Wave reports), and strategic advisory services. Revenue peaked at ~$530M in 2022 before declining to ~$397M in 2025, with a projected further drop to $345-360M in 2026.
disruption: AI and LLMs are commoditizing the premium market research and advisory services that Forrester sells. ChatGPT and other AI tools can now summarize markets, compare vendors, analyze competitive landscapes, and generate strategic recommendations—core capabilities that Forrester's analyst reports provide. Revenue declined 8% in 2025 to $397M, with consulting down 9% and events down 29%. The company posted a $119M net loss in 2025 (including goodwill impairment), is sunsetting its strategy consulting business entirely, cut 8% of its workforce, and projects a further 9-13% revenue decline in 2026. Stock has fallen 81% from $34.03 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$6.43. As one industry observer noted, AI has destroyed the informational advantage that made premium analyst research valuable—the 'research factory' middle of the analyst market is being automated. While Forrester is launching its own AI product (AI Access), the structural shift from expensive human-curated research to AI-generated insights represents a permanent compression of the market research industry's pricing power.

name: RWS Holdings plc
description: UK-based provider of language services, translation, localization, and intellectual property (patent) services. One of the world's largest translation companies with operations in 100+ languages. Market-leading Trados translation software.
disruption: AI-powered machine translation (DeepL, Google Translate, ChatGPT) has commoditized basic translation work that was RWS's bread-and-butter. Stock down 57% YoY. Adjusted profit fell 43% in FY2025. Took £88M goodwill impairment on Language Services division. Price pressure from clients expecting AI-level efficiency at lower cost. The company is attempting a pivot to 'AI-enabled content solutions' but margins are compressing during the transition.

name: Globant SA
description: Argentina-founded global digital and cognitive transformation company providing AI-driven software development, digital product engineering, and technology consulting. Operates nearshore delivery centers across Latin America (Argentina, Colombia, Brazil, Mexico, Uruguay) and increasingly in India and Eastern Europe. ~29,000 employees serving enterprise clients in financial services, media, technology, and consumer sectors. Revenue peaked at ~$2.45B in 2025. Market cap ~$1.9B, down from ~$8B at its 2021 peak.
disruption: AI coding tools (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude) are structurally reducing demand for the outsourced software development that is Globant's core business. Enterprises can accomplish more with fewer external developers, compressing the headcount-based engagements that drive revenue. Stock has fallen 74% from $174.22 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$44.95. Revenue growth collapsed from 20%+ annual rates pre-ChatGPT to essentially flat in 2025 (~1% growth), with Q4 2025 revenue declining 4.7% YoY. The company missed Q4 2025 earnings estimates and guided for just 0.2-2.2% revenue growth in 2026. Globant is attempting to pivot from traditional time-and-materials software delivery to an AI-native 'Pods' model—small teams of engineers augmented by AI agents using a token-based subscription pricing model rather than per-seat billing. AI Pods reached a $20.6M exit-rate ARR in 2025 with 45-60% gross margins, and management targets $60-100M by year-end 2026. However, this nascent AI revenue cannot yet offset the structural erosion of traditional delivery contracts. The broader IT services sector rout—driven by fears that AI permanently shrinks the total addressable market for outsourced software development—has hit Globant especially hard given its premium historical valuation and pure-play exposure to digital engineering work.

name: Five9 Inc
description: Cloud contact center software provider offering intelligent virtual agents (IVA), automatic call distribution, IVR, omnichannel routing, workforce management, and analytics for enterprise customer service operations. The leading independent CCaaS (Contact Center as a Service) platform with ~3,000 customers. Revenue peaked at ~$1B run rate in 2023. Stock peaked above $200 in 2021.
disruption: AI agents are structurally shrinking Five9's addressable market. The company's core revenue model is per-seat licensing—charging enterprises for each human agent using its contact center software. As AI virtual agents and chatbots replace human contact center staff, the total number of seats enterprises need declines, directly compressing Five9's revenue base. Stock has fallen 71% from $60.23 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$17.71. The stock dropped 50% in 2025 alone and hit a 52-week low of $16.10 in February 2026. Revenue growth decelerated from 30%+ to single digits (~8% in Q4 2025) as enterprises slow contact center hiring in favor of AI alternatives. The company is attempting to pivot to AI agent pricing (~$40-50/month per AI agent) and usage-based billing, but faces a painful cannibalization dynamic: if one AI agent replaces five human seats, Five9 must grow AI revenue 5x faster than seat revenue declines. Enterprise AI bookings doubled YoY and AI ARR reached $100M, but this cannot yet offset the structural erosion of the per-seat model. Competitors like Amazon Connect, Google CCAI, and AI-native startups are also pressuring Five9's position as the contact center shifts from human-operated software to AI-first automation.

name: WPP PLC
description: World's largest advertising and marketing services holding company, owning agencies like GroupM, Ogilvy, and AKQA. Provides advertising, media buying, PR, branding, and digital marketing services globally.
disruption: Generative AI can produce ad copy, creative assets, media plans, and marketing content that WPP's agencies currently charge premium rates to create. Clients are already using AI tools to reduce agency scope and headcount needs. WPP's stock has underperformed as investors price in margin compression from AI-driven creative automation. The company is investing heavily in AI tools but faces the fundamental threat that AI makes some agency services less valuable.

name: Concentrix Corp
description: Global technology-enabled business process outsourcing (BPO) company providing customer experience solutions, digital engineering, and technology services. ~300,000 employees across 40+ countries. Merged with Webhelp in 2023.
disruption: AI-powered chatbots, virtual agents, and automated customer service solutions threaten the labor-intensive customer support outsourcing model. Enterprise clients are increasingly deploying AI to handle routine inquiries that human agents currently process. Revenue growth has slowed significantly, and the market is pricing in structural displacement of call center labor. The company's massive workforce is its biggest asset and its biggest liability in an AI world.

name: Clarivate PLC
description: Global provider of research analytics, intellectual property intelligence, and scientific data services. Owns Web of Science (academic citation database), Derwent (patent analytics), CompuMark (trademark research), and LifeSci/Healthcare data platforms. Serves researchers, universities, law firms, pharma companies, and governments. Revenue of ~$2.46B in 2025. Market cap ~$1.7B, down from ~$12B at peak.
disruption: AI is commoditizing the premium research synthesis, patent analysis, and IP intelligence that Clarivate packages and sells at subscription prices. ChatGPT and AI research tools can now perform literature reviews, summarize scientific papers, analyze patent landscapes, and generate competitive intelligence reports—core use cases for Clarivate's Web of Science and Derwent platforms. Stock has fallen 71% from $9.17 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$2.62. Revenue declined from $2.63B (2023) to $2.56B (2024) to $2.46B (2025), with transactional revenue down 11.9% in Q4 2025 as customers shift to AI-powered alternatives for one-off research tasks. 2026 guidance projects a further decline to $2.30-2.42B with continued net losses ($124-189M). RBC Capital explicitly cited 'ongoing AI risks' when lowering its price target from $5 to $3 in February 2026. The company is attempting to pivot by embedding AI into its own products (Clarivate Nexus AI assistant, Derwent Patent Monitor with AI threat analysis, partnership with Anthropic's Claude), but faces the fundamental challenge that AI makes the act of synthesizing and searching research literature—Clarivate's core value proposition—dramatically cheaper and more accessible outside its walled-garden platforms.

name: ManpowerGroup Inc
description: Global workforce solutions and staffing company providing temporary and permanent recruitment, talent management, and workforce consulting across 75+ countries. One of the world's largest staffing firms.
disruption: AI automates recruiting workflows (screening, matching, scheduling) that staffing firms charge for, compressing margins. AI-driven workforce analytics reduce employer reliance on staffing intermediaries. Longer-term, AI and automation reduce overall demand for the white-collar temporary staffing that represents a significant portion of revenue. Revenue has been flat-to-declining as clients leverage internal AI tools for hiring.

name: Robert Half Inc
description: Staffing and consulting firm specializing in accounting, finance, technology, legal, and administrative professional placements. Also operates Protiviti consulting subsidiary. Focused on white-collar professional staffing.
disruption: AI directly threatens Robert Half's core business of placing white-collar professionals. AI coding tools reduce demand for contract programmers; AI accounting tools reduce demand for temp finance staff; AI legal research tools reduce demand for contract lawyers. Revenue has declined significantly from post-COVID peaks. The professional staffing segment is among the most exposed to AI displacement because the tasks are largely knowledge-based and automatable.

name: Fiverr International Ltd
description: Online marketplace connecting businesses with freelancers offering digital services including graphic design, copywriting, programming, video editing, and marketing. Platform for gig economy knowledge work.
disruption: AI tools directly replace many of the freelance services sold on Fiverr: AI can now write copy, generate logos, create marketing content, code simple applications, and produce videos. This threatens both the volume of freelance gigs and the pricing power of freelancers. The platform faces compression from both sides—fewer buyers need freelancers for AI-automatable tasks, and freelancers using AI tools face pricing pressure.

name: Shutterstock Inc
description: Global marketplace for licensed stock photographs, vectors, illustrations, videos, and music. Library of 900M+ images. Being acquired by Getty Images in $3.7B deal announced Jan 2025.
disruption: Same dynamic as Getty: AI image generators (DALL-E, Midjourney, Adobe Firefly) directly replace demand for stock photos, especially for generic/commercial use cases. Shutterstock's core download revenue is declining, masked by ~$100M in one-off AI data licensing deals (e.g., $250M OpenAI deal through 2027). Once these licensing deals are normalized, the underlying business erosion becomes visible.

name: BILL Holdings Inc
description: Cloud-based financial operations platform providing accounts payable automation, accounts receivable, spend management, and payment processing for small and mid-sized businesses. Processes ~$95B in payment volume quarterly across ~498,500 businesses. Also offers corporate cards and expense management. Revenue of ~$1.5B in FY2025 (+13% YoY), with Q2 FY2026 total revenue of $414.7M (+14% YoY). Market cap ~$4B, down from ~$30B at its 2021 peak. ~2,400 employees.
disruption: AI-native fintech competitors are building from-scratch autonomous AP workflows that structurally undercut BILL's legacy platform. Ramp launched 'Agents for AP'—specialized AI agents for invoice coding, fraud detection, approval routing, and payment optimization—that process invoices with 7x fewer clicks than BILL and close books two days faster. In early access, Ramp's AI agents flagged over $1M in fraudulent invoices in just 90 days. The competitive moat that BILL built over nearly two decades of SMB fintech is eroding as AI collapses the complexity of AP/AR workflows: invoice OCR, GL coding, approval chains, and payment routing are all becoming AI-automated commodities. Stock has fallen 65% from $114.67 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$40.24. Multiple analyst downgrades in early 2026: Morgan Stanley cut from Overweight to Equalweight, KBW lowered its price target to $46, and Truist reduced targets citing lower net customer additions. The broader 2026 SaaS selloff—driven by fears that AI agents will break per-seat and per-transaction pricing models—has hit BILL particularly hard as investors question whether a legacy AP platform can defend against AI-native competitors offering faster, cheaper, and more intelligent financial automation. BILL is deploying its own 'agentic AI' features on its platform, but faces the classic incumbent's dilemma: its revenue model depends on the manual financial workflows that AI is designed to eliminate.

name: Perion Network Ltd
description: Israeli digital advertising technology company specializing in search advertising distribution, display/video advertising, and publisher monetization. Built its business on a lucrative partnership with Microsoft Bing's search distribution marketplace, which at peak contributed ~35% of revenue. Revenue hit $743M in 2023 before collapsing. Market cap ~$364M.
disruption: A casualty of Microsoft's AI pivot in search. When Microsoft integrated Copilot into Bing and restructured its search advertising ecosystem for an AI-first world, it fundamentally changed the search distribution marketplace that Perion depended on. In Q1 2024, Bing implemented ad pricing and mechanism changes that reduced Perion's search advertising activity. Then in June 2024, Microsoft excluded Perion and other publishers from the Bing search distribution marketplace entirely. Search revenue from Bing went from ~35% of total revenue to under 5% in H2 2024. The Bing contract was not renewed at year-end 2024. Stock fell 68% from $26.60 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$8.57. Full-year 2024 revenue dropped 33% and the company swung to a net loss. Perion is pivoting to AI-driven advertising solutions (CTV, retail media, DOOH) but the core search monetization business was destroyed by Microsoft's AI transformation of Bing.

name: Asana Inc
description: Cloud-based work management and project collaboration platform providing task tracking, project timelines, workflow automation, goals tracking, and team coordination tools. Serves ~150,000 paying customers from SMBs to enterprises including Amazon, Google, and Johnson & Johnson. Founded by Facebook co-founder Dustin Moskovitz. Revenue of ~$723M in FY2026 (ending Jan 2026). Market cap ~$1.5B, down from ~$28B at its 2021 peak.
disruption: AI agents and copilots are structurally undermining the value of standalone project management SaaS. AI can now autonomously create project plans, assign tasks, generate status updates, triage incoming requests, and manage workflows—core functions that justify Asana's per-seat pricing. The per-seat model is under direct threat as AI 'teammates' handle work that previously required human project managers, compressing the number of seats enterprises need. Stock has fallen 60% from $17.21 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$6.86, and is down 92% from its 2021 peak of ~$145. Net retention rate dropped to 95-96%—below 100%, meaning existing customers are actively downsizing—reflecting persistent churn and downgrades across enterprise and mid-market segments. Revenue growth decelerated from 45%+ (FY2023) to ~9% (Q4 FY2026), with Q4 FY2026 revenue of $205.6M missing analyst consensus. Co-founder CEO Dustin Moskovitz stepped down in late 2025. Asana was named alongside Sprout Social and Five9 in Jefferies' 'SaaSpocalypse' report as SaaS companies most exposed to AI-driven seat compression. The company is pivoting aggressively with AI Studio (no-code AI workflow builder) and AI Teammates (autonomous agents), but faces a cannibalization paradox: the better its AI features work, the fewer human seats customers need. Meanwhile, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, and AI-native competitors are embedding project management capabilities directly into productivity suites, threatening Asana's standalone value proposition.

name: Paycom Software Inc
description: Cloud-based human capital management (HCM) platform providing payroll processing, talent acquisition, time and labor management, HR administration, and benefits enrollment through a single-database architecture. Serves ~37,000 clients, primarily mid-market US employers. Revenue of ~$2.05B in FY2025 (+11% YoY). ~7,000 employees (after 500+ AI-driven layoffs in Oct 2025). Market cap ~$6B, down from ~$22B at its 2021 peak.
disruption: AI is compressing the per-employee SaaS pricing model that underpins Paycom's business from multiple directions. Morningstar explicitly cut its fair value estimate from $208 to $130 and raised its uncertainty rating to Very High, citing 'developing risks posed by AI' and 'a higher likelihood of increased competition and lower seat counts due to clients seeing AI-driven efficiencies.' As AI automates HR administration, payroll processing, benefits enrollment, and employee onboarding, companies need fewer HR staff—directly reducing the per-employee-per-month seats that generate Paycom's revenue. Stock has fallen 60% from $314.73 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$124.82. Revenue growth collapsed from 30% (2021) to 6-7% projected for 2026, with guidance of $2.175-2.195B missing analyst estimates of $2.23B and triggering a 7.9% single-day drop. Paycom itself laid off 500+ employees in October 2025, explicitly citing AI-driven automation—an ironic signal that the same AI efficiencies reducing its own headcount are also shrinking its customers' HR departments. The company faces a compounding 'automation paradox': its flagship Beti product, which empowers employees to process their own payroll, has been so effective at eliminating errors that it cannibalized Paycom's traditional service revenue from fixing those errors. Jefferies cut its price target from $190 to $130. While Paycom is investing in AI features (IWant natural language engine, global HCM expansion to 180+ countries), it faces intensifying competition from AI-native HR platforms and embedded payroll offerings from giants like ADP, Workday, and SAP that are integrating AI capabilities directly into broader enterprise suites.

name: EPAM Systems Inc
description: Global leader in digital platform engineering and software development outsourcing. Headquartered in Pennsylvania with ~55,000 engineers across 50+ countries including major delivery centers in Eastern Europe, India, and Latin America. Revenue of ~$5.5B in 2025. Serves Fortune 500 clients across financial services, healthcare, life sciences, and technology sectors.
disruption: The bellwether for AI disruption of the IT services outsourcing industry. AI coding tools (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude) enable enterprise clients to accomplish more software development with fewer outsourced engineers, directly threatening the headcount-based billing model. Stock fell 59% from ~$344 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$131 in Feb 2026. Despite beating Q4 2025 earnings estimates, stock crashed 21% in a single day (Feb 20, 2026) after management guided for just 4.5-7.5% revenue growth in 2026—a dramatic deceleration from 15% in 2025. AI-related revenues ($600M projected for 2026) are growing but cannot offset the structural decline in traditional time-and-materials software development contracts. The broader 2026 software selloff has amplified fears that AI will permanently shrink the total addressable market for outsourced IT services.

name: ASGN Inc
description: Provider of IT staffing and professional services including technology consulting, digital transformation, and government IT solutions. Places IT professionals in contract and permanent roles across commercial and government sectors.
disruption: AI coding tools (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude) reduce demand for contract programmers and IT consultants. Enterprises can accomplish more with fewer developers, reducing the volume of IT staffing engagements. AI-assisted IT operations reduce need for temporary IT support staff. The IT staffing segment is particularly exposed because software development and IT support are among the tasks most enhanced by AI productivity tools.

name: Coursera Inc
description: Online learning platform offering courses, certificates, and degree programs from universities and companies worldwide. Provides both consumer and enterprise learning content. ~150M+ registered learners.
disruption: AI chatbots can now provide personalized tutoring, answer questions, and teach concepts interactively—competing with Coursera's pre-recorded lecture model. Free AI tools make much of Coursera's general education content less differentiated. AI also enables competitors to create courses faster and cheaper. While Coursera has integrated AI features, the fundamental value proposition of 'access to expert knowledge' is being commoditized by AI.

name: TripAdvisor Inc
description: Global online travel platform and reviews aggregator with over 1 billion reviews and opinions covering hotels, restaurants, experiences, and attractions. Historically the world's largest travel guidance site, built on search-driven traffic from Google. Revenue of ~$1.9B in 2025. Monthly visits dropped from 146-169M in early 2023 to ~120M by early 2025. Cut ~20% of workforce (~450 people) in 2025 restructuring.
disruption: Google AI Overviews and AI-powered travel planning tools are destroying TripAdvisor's core traffic engine. The company's legacy business model depends on users arriving via Google search to read reviews and click through to book hotels—but AI now answers travel queries directly in search results, eliminating the click-through. CEO Matt Goldberg explicitly cited 'ongoing declines in flyby visitors due to the changing search landscape and the rise of AI overviews,' while CMO Matthew Dacey warned that 'Google's AI mode in search is going to eat large chunks of search.' Stock has fallen ~49% from $19.58 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$10.07. The hotel metasearch segment—the legacy core—declined 14% in Q4 2025, with management guiding for continued mid-to-high teens revenue declines in Hotels & Other through 2026. Zero-click searches now account for 69% of all queries, and AI Overviews jumped from 10-15% of search results to nearly 60% in 2025. The company is pivoting to Experiences (Viator) and dining (TheFork), which now comprise two-thirds of revenue, but the original reviews-and-metasearch business that defined TripAdvisor is in structural decline. Activist investors Starboard Value and Palliser Capital are pushing for asset sales or a full company sale, signaling that the standalone public company model may not survive the AI-driven erosion of search-referral travel traffic.

name: Ziff Davis Inc
description: Global digital media company owning technology and lifestyle brands including PCMag, Mashable, Lifehacker, IGN, and Offers.com. Business model heavily reliant on search-driven affiliate commerce—users arrive via Google search, read product reviews, and click affiliate links to purchase. Also operates Ookla (Speedtest) and health/martech divisions. Revenue ~$1.4B in 2025. Announced $1.2B sale of Connectivity division (Ookla) to Accenture in March 2026.
disruption: AI-powered search is destroying Ziff Davis's core revenue engine. Google AI Overviews and ChatGPT now answer product comparison and tech recommendation queries directly, eliminating the high-intent search traffic that drove users to PCMag reviews and affiliate purchase links. The Technology & Shopping segment—the company's largest—declined 18% YoY in Q4 2025 ($108.9M vs $132.9M), and management expects continued double-digit declines in 2026. CEO Vivek Shah acknowledged that AI search traffic loss is essentially irreplaceable: the high-intent consumers who arrived via search to click through and purchase are simply gone. Stock has fallen 52% from $88.36 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$42.41 (and was as low as ~$27 before a temporary bounce from the Ookla asset sale). Q4 2025 earnings missed estimates, triggering a 12% single-day drop. The $1.2B Connectivity sale to Accenture—at a price exceeding the company's entire pre-deal market cap—signals management is liquidating its healthiest non-AI-disrupted assets to return capital, tacitly conceding that the core digital media business faces structural AI headwinds.

name: Kelly Services Inc Class A
description: Staffing company providing workforce solutions in office, professional, industrial, science, engineering, technology, and education sectors. One of the original temporary staffing agencies, founded 1946.
disruption: Same structural AI headwinds facing the entire staffing industry: AI automates the recruiting/matching process that staffing firms monetize, and AI/automation reduces overall demand for temporary workers. Kelly's mix of office/clerical temp staffing is particularly exposed as AI handles more administrative and data-entry tasks. Science and engineering segments have some insulation but professional staffing broadly faces displacement risk.

name: Telecom plus PLC
description: UK multi-utility provider offering bundled gas, electricity, broadband, mobile, and insurance services through a network marketing (partner) model. Trades as Utility Warehouse.
disruption: AI-powered comparison platforms and automated switching services reduce the value of Telecom Plus's human partner sales network. AI chatbots and virtual assistants can handle the customer acquisition and service roles that human partners currently perform. The network marketing model—relying on human relationships for sales—becomes less defensible as AI can match or exceed human product recommendations and customer service.

name: TaskUs Inc
description: Global digital outsourcing company providing content moderation (trust & safety), customer experience, and AI data annotation services to major tech companies. Largest client is Meta (~27% of revenue). Operations across 28 countries with ~47,000 employees. Named a Leader in Everest Group's Data Annotation and Labeling PEAK Matrix. Revenue hit a record $1.18B in 2025. Market cap ~$900M, down from ~$3B at its 2021 peak.
disruption: AI is disrupting TaskUs from multiple directions simultaneously. Its largest client, Meta (27% of revenue), has explicitly signaled it will leverage AI to automate content moderation and reduce outsourced work volumes in 2026—directly contributing to TaskUs's guidance of just 3.5% revenue growth, a dramatic deceleration from 19% in 2025. AI-powered moderation tools can increasingly handle the trust & safety work (flagging harmful content, financial crime compliance) that human moderators perform, and management acknowledged trust & safety will likely have the lowest growth rate in 2026 'given the impact that automation is likely to have on core content moderation volumes.' Stock has fallen 45% from $18.73 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$10.20, and dropped 39% in the six months following record 2025 earnings as investors priced in structural AI headwinds. TaskUs faces a deeper paradox in its AI data annotation business: while AI Services grew 46% in 2025, the improving capability of AI models means they increasingly need less human-labeled training data (via synthetic data, self-supervision, and RLHF improvements), potentially shrinking the very segment that is supposed to offset legacy declines. The company is investing $25M+ in AI transformation and pivoting to outcome-based pricing, but the fundamental tension remains—TaskUs's human-labor-intensive model is being squeezed by the same AI technology it helps train and moderate.

name: Freshworks Inc
description: Cloud-based customer support and IT service management (ITSM) SaaS platform offering helpdesk ticketing, CRM, and IT operations tools for small and mid-market businesses. Products include Freshdesk (customer support), Freshservice (ITSM), and Freshsales (CRM). ~4,500 employees. Revenue of ~$839M in FY2025 with ~$907M ARR. Market cap ~$2.3B, down from ~$12B at IPO peak in 2021.
disruption: A poster child of the February 2026 'SaaSpocalypse'—the $285B+ enterprise software selloff triggered by fears that AI agents will structurally break the per-seat SaaS business model. AI chatbots and autonomous agents can now resolve customer support tickets end-to-end, reducing the number of human agents who need Freshworks' helpdesk software licenses. AI coding tools ('vibe coding') enable businesses to build custom support workflows without buying off-the-shelf SaaS. Jefferies downgraded FRSH to Hold, specifically citing AI disruption risk to the customer experience business. Piper Sandler also downgraded on fears of seat compression as AI replaces human support agents. Stock has fallen 41% from $14.05 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$8.23, and is down 95% from its 2021 IPO peak. Revenue growth has decelerated from 20%+ (2023) to ~14% (2025), with 2026 profit guidance missing estimates. While Freshworks is investing in its own AI agent platform (Freddy AI, achieving 70% ticket deflection across 5,000+ organizations), the fundamental threat is that AI makes the per-seat helpdesk model less valuable—if one AI agent handles the work of five human agents, the total seats needed shrinks dramatically, compressing the revenue base for all customer support SaaS vendors.

name: Wolters Kluwer NV
description: Global information services company providing software and expert solutions for healthcare, tax & accounting, financial services, compliance, and legal professionals. Deep domain expertise embedded in workflow tools.
disruption: AI threatens to commoditize the expert knowledge that Wolters Kluwer has historically packaged and sold at premium prices. AI can now perform legal research, tax calculations, regulatory compliance checks, and clinical decision support—core Wolters Kluwer use cases. However, WKL is better positioned than most on this list because its products are deeply embedded in professional workflows and it's actively integrating AI into its own platforms.

name: LegalZoom.com Inc
description: Online legal technology platform providing business formation, legal document preparation, registered agent services, intellectual property filing, and compliance solutions for small businesses and individuals.
disruption: AI can now draft legal documents, file business formations, answer legal questions, and handle compliance tasks that LegalZoom charges for. ChatGPT and specialized legal AI tools provide much of LegalZoom's core service at near-zero marginal cost. The company's value proposition of 'affordable legal services for small businesses' is undercut when AI makes those same services essentially free.

name: H & R Block Inc
description: Tax preparation company offering assisted and DIY tax filing services through retail offices, online platforms, and software. Serves ~20M tax returns annually. Also offers financial products and small business services.
disruption: AI can now prepare basic to moderately complex tax returns conversationally, threatening both H&R Block's retail tax prep locations (where humans prepare returns) and its DIY software (where AI chatbots provide a more intuitive experience). ChatGPT and similar tools can answer most routine tax questions that drive customers to H&R Block. The IRS's free Direct File program combined with AI assistance further erodes the value proposition.

name: Adobe Inc
description: Software giant providing creative tools (Photoshop, Illustrator, Premiere Pro), document management (Acrobat), and digital marketing solutions. Dominant position in professional creative workflows.
disruption: AI image and video generation tools (Midjourney, DALL-E, Runway) threaten Adobe's creative software monopoly by enabling non-designers to create professional content. While Adobe has launched Firefly (its own AI generator), the risk is that AI reduces the need for complex creative software altogether—why learn Photoshop when AI generates the image directly? The stock trades at a premium that may not be justified if AI compresses the total addressable market for creative tools.

name: Teradata Corp
description: Legacy enterprise data warehousing and analytics platform providing large-scale data management, integration, and analytics solutions to Fortune 500 companies. Founded in 1979, Teradata was once the gold standard for enterprise data warehousing. Revenue of ~$1.66B in FY2025, down ~5% YoY. ~7,000 employees. Market cap ~$3B, down from a 2022 high of ~$5B.
disruption: AI and cloud-native analytics platforms are structurally displacing Teradata's legacy data warehousing business. Cloud-native competitors like Snowflake, Databricks, and Google BigQuery offer built-in AI/ML capabilities, serverless scaling, and modern architectures that make Teradata's on-premise roots a liability. Revenue has declined for multiple consecutive years—from $1.83B in FY2023 to $1.75B in FY2024 to $1.66B in FY2025—as enterprise customers migrate AI and analytics workloads to platforms purpose-built for the generative AI era. Stock has fallen 19% from $32.95 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$26.83. Q4 2024 revenue missed estimates at $409M, down 10.5% YoY, triggering a 20% single-day stock drop. Teradata's cloud ARR grew 11%, but not fast enough to offset accelerating on-premise attrition. The company has over 150 AI customer engagements and launched AI-focused products (AI Factory, Enterprise Vector Store, AgentBuilder), but faces the classic legacy vendor dilemma: its installed base is migrating to the same cloud-native AI platforms it's trying to compete with. Analysts project revenue will continue declining ~1% annually through 2028. Multiple analysts have noted that the generative AI spending cycle is being captured by cloud-native and open-source competitors rather than legacy data platform vendors.

name: Genpact Ltd
description: Global professional services firm providing business process management and IT services, with focus on digital transformation, analytics, and enterprise AI. Serves Fortune 500 companies across finance, insurance, and manufacturing.
disruption: AI automates the business processes (accounting, insurance claims processing, supply chain management) that Genpact's workforce of ~125,000 handles for enterprise clients. The business process outsourcing model is threatened when AI can perform rules-based processing, data entry, and document analysis faster and cheaper than offshore labor. Genpact is pivoting to AI implementation consulting but faces the classic innovator's dilemma.

name: Omnicom Group Inc
description: Global advertising and marketing holding company owning agencies including BBDO, DDB, TBWA, and FleishmanHillard. Provides advertising, strategic media planning, CRM, PR, and specialty marketing services. Announced merger with IPG.
disruption: Same dynamics as WPP: generative AI can produce ad creative, media plans, and marketing content faster and cheaper. Clients are exploring AI-first creative workflows that reduce reliance on agency labor. The advertising holding company model faces margin pressure as AI tools enable in-house marketing teams to handle work previously outsourced to agencies. The Omnicom-IPG merger is partly a defensive consolidation against this AI-driven disruption.

name: Upwork Inc
description: Global freelancing platform connecting businesses with independent professionals for projects in development, design, writing, marketing, and administrative work. Largest platform for remote freelance work.
disruption: AI tools directly displace freelance work in writing, design, basic coding, data entry, and marketing—the highest-volume categories on Upwork. Businesses can use AI instead of hiring a freelancer for many routine knowledge tasks. Upwork has seen pressure on take rates and gross services volume. Like Fiverr, the platform faces a shrinking middle market where tasks are too simple for human freelancers (AI handles them) but the complex work remains human-intensive.

name: BuzzFeed Inc
description: Digital media company known for listicles, quizzes, news, and entertainment content. Operates BuzzFeed, HuffPost, and Tasty brands. Revenue driven by advertising and content partnerships.
disruption: AI-generated content has flooded the web, directly competing with BuzzFeed's core output of listicles, quizzes, and lightweight articles. Google AI Overviews and AI-powered search reduce click-through traffic to publishers, cratering pageviews and ad revenue. Advertisers increasingly use AI tools to produce branded content in-house rather than partnering with publishers. BuzzFeed's own pivot to AI-generated content (AI quizzes) failed to offset the structural decline. The economics of ad-supported digital media have deteriorated as AI commoditizes content creation.

name: Gartner Inc
description: World's leading IT research and advisory firm providing syndicated research, consulting, and conferences to enterprise technology leaders. Gartner's Magic Quadrants, Hype Cycles, and analyst insights shape billions in IT spending decisions. Revenue of ~$6.3B in FY2025 with ~20,000 employees.
disruption: AI is commoditizing the research and advisory services that enterprises pay Gartner premium prices for. Large language models can now synthesize vast amounts of market data, vendor comparisons, and strategic recommendations—tasks that previously required expensive Gartner analyst subscriptions. The 2026 software sell-off hit Gartner particularly hard: Q4 2025 results missed revenue expectations and 2026 guidance came in below analyst projections, triggering a 20%+ single-day drop. The stock has fallen over 50% from its pre-ChatGPT price as investors price in the risk that AI tools fundamentally compress the addressable market for premium IT research. Like Forrester, Gartner faces the existential question of whether enterprises will continue paying for human analyst opinions when AI can provide comparable insights at a fraction of the cost.

name: Sprinklr Inc
description: Unified customer experience management platform that helps enterprises manage social media, customer care, marketing, and engagement across 30+ digital channels. Serves large brands with tools for social listening, content publishing, advertising, and customer service. Revenue of ~$800M with ~4,500 employees.
disruption: AI agents and LLM-powered chatbots are rapidly replacing the multi-channel customer engagement workflows that Sprinklr's platform orchestrates. Enterprises increasingly deploy AI-native solutions for social media monitoring, sentiment analysis, and customer service—capabilities that were once Sprinklr's core value proposition. AI tools commoditize social listening and content generation, reducing the need for expensive unified CX platforms. Competitors like Salesforce and ServiceNow have embedded generative AI directly into their CRM and service management suites, eroding Sprinklr's differentiation. The company's growth has stalled as customers consolidate onto AI-enhanced platforms rather than maintaining standalone CX tools, and the stock has fallen nearly 30% below its pre-ChatGPT price.

name: Conduent Incorporated
description: Global business process services company spun off from Xerox in 2017, providing outsourced digital interactions, transaction processing, and government services. Operates in Commercial, Government, and Transportation segments. Services include HR administration, benefits management, toll collection, payment processing, and customer care. Revenue of ~$3B with ~55,000 employees across 20+ countries.
disruption: AI automation is displacing the human-intensive business process work that forms Conduent's core revenue base. AI-powered document processing, intelligent automation, and chatbots directly replace the manual transaction processing, claims handling, and customer interactions Conduent performs for government and enterprise clients. CEO Harshita Agadi acknowledged that clients may take AI-driven processes in-house, and the company is attempting to pivot by partnering with AI disruptors rather than building its own. Revenue declined ~8% YoY excluding acquisitions in Q4 2025, the company posted a net loss of $170M, and stock has fallen ~68% from its pre-ChatGPT level. The structural threat is clear: the BPO industry's FTE-based billing model is giving way to outcome-based pricing that rewards automation over headcount, with FTE-based contracts projected to fall from 42% to 28% of industry revenue within three years.

name: DoubleVerify Holdings Inc
description: Leading digital ad verification and brand safety platform that helps advertisers ensure their ads are seen by real people in safe, suitable environments. Measures viewability, fraud, and brand safety across display, video, social, and CTV channels. Analyzes billions of ad impressions daily for major advertisers and platforms including Meta, Google, and The Trade Desk. Revenue of ~$733M with ~1,800 employees.
disruption: AI is disrupting DoubleVerify's core ad verification business from multiple directions. Generative AI models from Anthropic and OpenAI can now automate complex software auditing and ad verification tasks that enterprises previously outsourced to specialized platforms, prompting investors to reassess long-term demand. The shift toward privacy-first, AI-driven, and cookieless advertising models undermines the relevance of traditional verification approaches. AI-generated content ('AI slop') is flooding the web at a scale that overwhelms conventional verification methods, while major ad platforms are building AI-native measurement directly into their ecosystems, reducing reliance on third-party verifiers. The stock has fallen over 60% from its pre-ChatGPT price as the market prices in the risk that AI commoditizes ad verification.

name: Unity Software Inc
description: World's most widely used game engine and real-time 3D development platform, powering 27% of all games globally. Provides tools for game development, AR/VR experiences, digital twins, and interactive 3D content. Also operates Unity Ads and the Unity Vector advertising platform. Revenue of ~$2B with ~8,000 employees.
disruption: AI-powered game development tools are threatening Unity's core game engine business. Google's Project Genie can generate interactive 3D worlds from simple text prompts, raising fears that accessible AI tools could democratize game creation and reduce reliance on traditional engines. The broader AI disruption narrative hit Unity especially hard in 2026: despite beating Q4 2025 estimates with $503M revenue (+10% YoY), weak Q1 2026 guidance ($480-490M vs. $494M expected) triggered a 33% single-day crash. The company is winding down its IronSource ad network—now viewed as commoditized—and pivoting to the AI-powered Unity Vector platform, creating a painful revenue transition gap. Stock has fallen over 52% from its pre-ChatGPT price as investors price in the existential risk that AI-generated content tools could bypass dedicated game engines entirely.

name: Atlassian Corporation Plc
description: Australian-American enterprise software company providing collaboration and project management tools including Jira, Confluence, Trello, and Bitbucket. Serves over 300,000 customers globally with a seat-based SaaS model. Revenue of ~$4.8B with ~12,000 employees.
disruption: AI agents and coding assistants are threatening Atlassian's seat-based SaaS model. As AI tools automate software development workflows, project management, and documentation tasks, enterprises need fewer human seats—directly eroding Atlassian's per-user pricing structure. Citi downgraded the stock in early 2026 on AI disruption fears, warning that AI agents could reduce the number of knowledge workers needing Jira and Confluence licenses. The company laid off 10% of its workforce (1,600 employees) in March 2026 to fund its AI pivot. Despite revenue still growing 23% YoY, the stock has cratered as investors price in the structural risk that AI automation compresses the total addressable market for seat-based collaboration tools.

name: Capgemini SE
description: French multinational IT services and consulting company, one of the world's largest. Provides consulting, technology services, digital transformation, and outsourcing across 50+ countries. Revenue of ~€22.5B with ~340,000 employees. Competes with Accenture, Infosys, TCS, and Wipro in enterprise IT services.
disruption: AI is structurally disrupting Capgemini's labor-intensive IT services model. Morningstar downgraded the company's moat rating to 'none' in 2026, citing that AI renders the core time-and-materials pricing model obsolete as the primary input—human labor—is being compressed by AI automation. Bookings declined 5% YoY in 2025 as clients delay spending to assess AI alternatives to traditional outsourced services. The company announced €700M in restructuring charges to pivot its workforce toward AI-enabled services, effectively acknowledging the disruption. Revenue growth missed analyst expectations for three consecutive quarters, and offshore pricing power has eroded as AI tools reduce the value proposition of labor arbitrage. The stock has fallen over 40% from its pre-ChatGPT price as investors price in the structural threat to the entire IT services industry.

name: Dassault Systèmes SE
description: French multinational software company specializing in 3D design, simulation, and product lifecycle management (PLM). Provides the 3DEXPERIENCE platform, CATIA, SOLIDWORKS, and other CAD/engineering tools used across aerospace, automotive, and manufacturing. Revenue of ~€6.4B with ~24,000 employees.
disruption: AI is disrupting Dassault's enterprise engineering software moat. Generative AI tools that can create 3D models, automate design workflows, and simulate products from text prompts threaten to commoditize traditional CAD/PLM software. The stock plunged 21% in a single day in February 2026—its worst drop in over two decades—after weak Q4 2025 results and anemic 2026 guidance of just 3-5% revenue growth. Software revenue was flat, transactional license sales fell 7%, and Life Sciences revenue declined 4%. The company is attempting a major pricing overhaul from per-seat licensing to AI usage-based pricing, creating a painful transition gap. Broader AI fears triggered a sector-wide sell-off of SaaS and data provider stocks, with Dassault among the hardest hit as investors questioned whether the moat around legacy engineering software remains defensible.

name: C3.ai Inc
description: Enterprise AI software company providing a platform for building and deploying AI applications across industries including energy, manufacturing, defense, and financial services. Founded in 2009 by Tom Siebel, IPO'd in December 2020 at $42/share. Revenue peaked at ~$266M in FY2023 before steep declines.
disruption: A cautionary tale of an AI company disrupted by better AI. Before ChatGPT, C3.ai was a leading enterprise AI platform—companies paid for its proprietary tools to build predictive maintenance, fraud detection, and supply chain optimization models. After the generative AI revolution, enterprises pivoted to foundation models from OpenAI, Anthropic, and cloud-native AI services (Azure AI, Google Vertex AI, AWS SageMaker) that offered superior capabilities at lower cost, commoditizing C3.ai's platform. Revenue collapsed 46% YoY in fiscal Q3 2026 ($53M vs $99M prior year), full-year guidance was slashed from $448-485M to under $251M, and the company cut 26% of its workforce. Palantir's AIP platform captured government/enterprise AI spend while hyperscaler tools captured the rest. Stock trades at all-time lows, down ~36% from its pre-ChatGPT price.

name: Infosys Limited
description: Indian multinational IT services and consulting company, one of the world's largest offshore IT outsourcers. Provides consulting, technology, and outsourcing services across 50+ countries. Revenue of ~$19B with ~320,000 employees. Competes with TCS, Wipro, Accenture, and Capgemini in enterprise IT services.
disruption: AI is structurally threatening Infosys's core offshore IT outsourcing model. Generative AI tools—especially coding assistants and AI automation platforms like Anthropic's Claude—can now perform tasks that historically formed the backbone of offshore IT services revenue: coding, testing, maintenance, and support workflows. The February 2026 AI sell-off hit Indian IT stocks particularly hard, with Infosys plunging 13% over two trading sessions and the Nifty IT index falling 32% from its all-time high. Jefferies warned that application services, which account for 40-70% of revenue for Indian IT firms, are vulnerable to AI-led disruption and that consensus growth estimates may not fully reflect these risks. JPMorgan's extreme bearish scenario assumes zero growth indefinitely due to AI disruption. Despite Infosys developing 90+ generative AI programs and laying out a $300-400B AI market opportunity roadmap, investors remain unconvinced the pivot can offset the structural threat to labor arbitrage-based IT services.

name: Similarweb Ltd
description: Israeli-founded digital intelligence company providing web analytics, competitive intelligence, and market research tools. Tracks website traffic, app usage, and digital marketing performance for enterprises worldwide. Revenue of ~$283M in 2025 with customers across eCommerce, finance, and digital marketing. Competes with Semrush, Ahrefs, and SimilarTech.
disruption: Generative AI is undermining Similarweb's core business from two directions. First, AI-powered 'zero-click' search tools like ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google AI Overviews are reducing traditional web traffic—the very signal Similarweb measures and monetizes. As users get answers directly from AI without visiting websites, Similarweb's browser-click and referral data loses predictive accuracy for eCommerce and marketing performance. Second, AI tools can now generate competitive intelligence and market analysis that previously required Similarweb's premium subscriptions. The company's net revenue retention slipped to 98% as customers face these headwinds. Similarweb is pivoting with AI Studio and selling data to LLM providers (11% of Q4 2025 revenue from AI-related deals), but delayed LLM data deals and self-cannibalization from transitioning customers from SEO to GEO (generative engine optimization) have pressured results. Stock fell 58% in the past year as the 2026 SaaSpocalypse sell-off compounded structural concerns about the future relevance of web traffic analytics in an AI-first world.

name: monday.com Ltd
description: Israeli-founded cloud-based Work OS platform for project management, team collaboration, and workflow automation. Serves 225,000+ customers across industries with products spanning work management, CRM, and dev tools. Revenue of ~$972M in 2025 (up 28% YoY) with ~2,200 employees. Competes with Atlassian, Asana, Smartsheet, and ClickUp.
disruption: AI agents are directly threatening monday.com's core business of manual project tracking, task management, and workflow coordination—exactly the repetitive, structured work that AI automates best. The February 2026 SaaSpocalypse triggered by Anthropic's Claude Cowork launch sent MNDY plunging 21% in a single day as investors reassessed the viability of seat-based SaaS pricing when AI agents can handle project coordination autonomously. The company's 2026 revenue guidance of 18-19% growth—a sharp deceleration from 28% in 2025—spooked growth investors who see the traditional work management category being subsumed by AI-native tools. Seeking Alpha published analysis titled 'monday.com Is Being Disrupted By AI,' and multiple analysts flagged that project management platforms face existential risk as enterprises shift budgets from per-seat software licenses to AI infrastructure. Despite management's claims of no AI impact and launch of AI features like agents and 'vibe' tools, the stock has fallen 30% below its pre-ChatGPT price as the market prices in structural disruption to the work management category.

name: Match Group Inc
description: Parent company of Tinder, Hinge, Match.com, OkCupid, and other dating platforms serving users across 190+ countries. Tinder remains the flagship product with ~10 million paying subscribers globally, though payer growth has stalled. Full-year 2025 revenue of ~$3.5B. Restructured in 2026 with 12% headcount reduction to fund AI investments. Market cap ~$8B, down from ~$40B at its 2021 peak.
disruption: AI is disrupting online dating from multiple angles. AI-generated fake profiles now flood dating platforms—Norton blocked 17 million dating scam attacks in Q4 2025 alone (+19% YoY), with AI enabling scammers to generate convincing photos and craft messages that fool the majority of recipients. This erodes the trust and authenticity dating apps depend on, accelerating 'swipe fatigue.' Simultaneously, AI companion apps (Replika, Character.ai, ChatGPT) are competing for the emotional connection users seek, with 25% of young adults believing AI could replace real romantic relationships. Gen Z—the critical growth demographic—is abandoning dating apps at alarming rates, with 79% dropping regular use. Match Group reported weakening payer growth as Gen Z shifts to AI-driven alternatives, and issued lackluster 2026 guidance of $3.41-3.54B vs. analyst estimates of $3.59B. The company is investing $60M in AI features for Tinder, but this creates a near-term monetization headwind. Stock has fallen 33% below its pre-ChatGPT price as the same AI forces devastating Bumble (-85%) compress the entire online dating category.

name: FactSet Research Systems Inc
description: Leading provider of financial data, analytics, and technology solutions for investment professionals. Offers integrated workstations and enterprise solutions used by portfolio managers, analysts, and investment bankers at ~8,200 client firms globally. Revenue of ~$2.2B (FY2025) with ~12,400 employees. Competes with Bloomberg, S&P Capital IQ, Refinitiv, and MSCI.
disruption: AI is threatening FactSet's premium financial data terminal business from multiple directions. Generative AI tools can now synthesize financial data, build models, and generate investment research that previously required expensive FactSet subscriptions—commoditizing the structured data and analytics that form FactSet's core value proposition. The February 2026 SaaSpocalypse triggered by Anthropic's Claude Cowork launch hit financial data stocks hard, with FactSet experiencing double-digit drops alongside Thomson Reuters and RELX. Asset management clients—FactSet's core customer base—are tightening budgets as AI-powered alternatives make financial data cheaper and more accessible. The stock has fallen 55% over the past 52 weeks and hit multi-year lows in early 2026 as investors reprice the entire financial data sector. Despite FactSet partnering with Anthropic to integrate its data into Claude and reporting solid Q1 FY2026 results (revenue up 6.9% YoY to $607.6M), the market is pricing in structural disruption to the premium financial data terminal model as AI democratizes access to financial intelligence.

name: GitLab Inc
description: Cloud-based DevSecOps platform providing source code management, CI/CD pipelines, security scanning, and project planning in a single application. Serves ~10,000 customers including ~1,100 with $100K+ ARR. Revenue of ~$760M (FY2026) growing ~28% YoY with ~2,300 employees. Competes with GitHub (Microsoft), Bitbucket (Atlassian), and emerging AI-native developer tools.
disruption: AI coding agents are disrupting GitLab's core DevOps platform business. Tools like GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude Code, and Devin are automating large portions of the software development lifecycle—from code generation to testing to deployment—reducing the number of human developer seats that enterprises need to license. OpenAI reportedly built an internal GitHub alternative, fueling fears it may 'productize' an AI-native repository that bypasses traditional DevOps platforms entirely. GitLab's FY2027 revenue guidance disappointed Wall Street, reflecting decelerating growth as enterprises reassess developer tooling budgets in an AI-first world. The February 2026 SaaSpocalypse hit GitLab hard, with the stock down 74% from all-time highs. Despite launching its own AI features (GitLab Duo Agent Platform) and a $400M share buyback program, the market is pricing in structural disruption to the per-seat DevOps model as AI agents handle more development tasks autonomously.

name: CS Disco Inc
description: Legal technology company providing cloud-based eDiscovery, document review, and case management software for law firms, corporations, and government agencies. Founded in 2012, IPO'd in 2021. Revenue of ~$165M (2025) with ~500 employees. Competes with Relativity, Reveal, Nuix, and emerging AI-native legal platforms like Harvey and Legora.
disruption: AI is disrupting CS Disco's core eDiscovery and document review business. Generative AI tools can now analyze, summarize, and review legal documents far faster and cheaper than Disco's traditional keyword-and-analytics approach, commoditizing the legal review workflows that drive Disco's revenue. AI-native legal startups like Harvey (valued at 110x ARR vs. Disco's ~1x) and Legora are attracting enterprise legal budgets—Disco's stock plunged 20% on news of Legora's launch. The February 2026 SaaSpocalypse hit legal tech particularly hard after Anthropic released legal-focused AI tools, triggering a repricing of the entire legal software sector. While Disco launched its own AI assistant (Cecilia AI) with 300%+ adoption growth, the company remains unprofitable and revenue growth (~13% YoY) lags AI-native competitors. The stock has fallen 48% below its pre-ChatGPT price as the market prices in structural disruption to traditional legal technology platforms.

name: NICE Ltd
description: Israeli-founded enterprise software company providing cloud and on-premises contact center solutions including workforce management, quality management, analytics, and AI-powered virtual agents. A market leader in the CCaaS (Contact Center as a Service) space alongside Genesys and Five9, serving ~25,000 organizations globally with ~$2.7B annual revenue and ~8,000 employees. Market cap ~$6.8B, down from a ~$20B peak in 2022.
disruption: AI chatbots and voice agents are structurally disrupting NICE's contact center software business. As enterprises deploy AI agents that handle customer interactions autonomously, demand for human contact center agents—and the per-seat software they use—is shrinking. NICE's stock hit its lowest level since 2019, falling 42.5% in 2025 alone as investors repriced the entire contact center software sector. The company spent $955M acquiring German AI-agent startup Cognigy to pivot toward AI-first customer service automation, but the acquisition triggered a weaker 2026 profitability forecast (operating margin guided down from 31% to 25–26%), spooking investors further. While NICE reports that 73% of its customers use some level of bot automation, only 14% of issues are resolved without human intervention—suggesting significant further AI displacement ahead. The February 2026 SaaSpocalypse compounded the decline as AI agents were seen as a direct threat to seat-based enterprise software models. AI-native startups and cloud providers (Amazon Connect, Google CCAI) are also pressuring NICE's market position from below.

name: Kforce Inc
description: Technology and finance & accounting staffing firm providing temporary and direct-hire professionals to Fortune 500 and mid-market clients across the US. Specializes in placing IT contractors including software developers, data scientists, project managers, and cybersecurity professionals. Revenue of ~$1.4B (2025, down 5.4% YoY) with ~2,000 internal employees placing ~8,000+ consultants. Competes with ASGN (Apex Group), Robert Half (RHI), ManpowerGroup (MAN), and other IT staffing firms.
disruption: AI coding tools and automation are structurally eroding demand for the contract IT workers that form Kforce's core business. GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude Code, and AI coding agents allow enterprises to accomplish more development work with fewer human developers, directly reducing the billable hours and headcount that IT staffing firms monetize. Kforce's 2025 revenue fell 5.4% and adjusted EPS dropped 22% as fewer consultants were placed despite stable bill rates—indicating volume erosion, not pricing pressure. The stock plunged 11.7% after Q4 2025 earnings and continued falling during the February 2026 SaaSpocalypse sell-off, which specifically targeted companies whose labor-intensive models are threatened by AI automation. Seeking Alpha highlighted 'AI Encroachment And Stagnant Labour Market Continues To Stifle Growth,' and the Staffing Industry Analysts projected temporary technology staffing to decline 2% in 2025. The stock now trades at a steep discount to historical multiples as the market prices in a structural shrinkage of the IT staffing TAM. Peer firms ASGN (-42%), Robert Half (-40%), and ManpowerGroup (-43%) are experiencing the same AI-driven headwinds.

name: Angi Inc
description: Home services marketplace formed from the 2017 merger of Angie's List and HomeAdvisor under IAC (rebranded to Angi in 2021). Connects homeowners with service professionals (contractors, plumbers, electricians) for home improvement, maintenance, and repair projects. Revenue of ~$1.03B in 2025, down 13% YoY. Approximately 2,800 employees before cutting 350 (~12.5%) in January 2026. Market cap ~$311M, down from over $4B at its 2021 peak.
disruption: Google AI Overviews and AI-powered search have directly crushed Angi's traffic-dependent business model—the same AI disruption devastating TripAdvisor and Ziff Davis. Angi's legacy revenue engine depends on homeowners arriving via Google search to read contractor reviews and submit service requests, but AI now answers 'who's the best plumber near me' type queries directly in search results, eliminating the click-through. Management explicitly cited 'continued declines in Google SEO and network channels' as central to the Q4 2025 revenue shortfall, as algorithm changes and AI Overviews have reduced free traffic. Organic click-through rates for informational queries with AI Overviews have fallen 61% since mid-2024. Simultaneously, users are increasingly turning to ChatGPT and Nextdoor for contractor recommendations, bypassing Angi's pay-to-play directory entirely. In January 2026, Angi announced 350 layoffs (~12.5% of workforce) explicitly citing 'AI-driven efficiency improvements' to save $70-80M annually—acknowledging that AI is reshaping both its cost structure and its top-line. Stock fell 65% on a split-adjusted basis from $20.60 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$7.20, and hit an all-time low of $6.43 in March 2026. The company executed a 1:10 reverse stock split in March 2025 and is attempting an AI-powered pivot (AI helper in service request flow with 35% homeowner engagement and 3.3x conversion), but faces the 'shrinking to profitability' dilemma as revenue continues declining faster than management can re-engineer the business for an AI-first search landscape.

name: Freelancer Limited
description: Sydney-headquartered operator of Freelancer.com, the world's largest freelance marketplace by user count with ~80M registered users across 247 countries and territories, plus Escrow.com (online payments) and Loadshift (Australian freight marketplace). Founder Matt Barrie remains CEO. FY2025 results delivered a 'return to profitability' driven mainly by Escrow.com and Loadshift rather than the core marketplace, with Q3 2025 group GMV collapsing 22.5% to $206.8M as the platform lapped an extraordinary prior-year transaction. Market cap of ~A$70M, trading near the bottom of its A$0.150-0.325 52-week range.
disruption: Freelancer's three highest-volume marketplace categories — writing, software/web development, and design — are exactly the categories where AI is automating work fastest. Industry data for 2025 shows writing project demand down ~30% YoY, software/web development down ~20%, and entry-level writing falling from 15% to under 9% of jobs — the same dynamic pressuring FVRR and UPWK on this list. Stock has fallen ~48% from A$0.26 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~A$0.135. The profit recovery in 2025 came almost entirely from adjacent non-marketplace businesses (Escrow.com and Loadshift), tacitly acknowledging that the core freelance platform is under structural pressure from AI. Management is leaning into AI as a category itself — positioning AI development as 'the next major wave after web and mobile' — and adding AI-assisted matching, quality scoring, and audio/video calling features to defend retention, but the same LLMs creating new AI engineering jobs are eliminating the much larger volume of routine writing, coding, and design gigs that built Freelancer's GMV. Founder Matt Barrie has been dollar-averaging into the weakness (673,076 shares bought on Dec 5, 2025 at ~A$0.23), a personal bet that the marketplace can survive the AI transition.

name: PagerDuty Inc
description: Digital operations management platform providing real-time incident alerting, on-call scheduling, event intelligence, and automated response orchestration for engineering and IT teams. Serves ~29,000 customers including over half the Fortune 500. Revenue of ~$499M (FY2026) growing just 2.7% YoY with ~1,000 employees. Achieved first full year of GAAP profitability in FY2026, but revenue growth has stalled. Competes with Opsgenie (Atlassian), Datadog Incident Management, and emerging AI-native operations tools.
disruption: AI agents are directly threatening PagerDuty's core incident management business. Autonomous AI agents can now auto-triage alerts, diagnose root causes, and remediate issues without human intervention — the exact workflow PagerDuty monetizes through per-seat pricing for on-call engineers. Anthropic's Managed Agents announcement triggered an 8.7% single-day drop as traders recognized that autonomous AI systems could replace human-operated incident response tools with more efficient, autonomous infrastructure. The February 2026 SaaSpocalypse compounded the decline, with PD falling 24.8% over 7 days and 36.8% over 30 days as investors repriced the entire seat-based SaaS model. Growing use of AI automation and open-source tools threatens PagerDuty's pricing power, market size, and customer retention. Despite expanding its AI ecosystem with 30+ partners and launching PagerDuty Advance agents for autonomous cloud remediation via MCP and API integrations, the market views PagerDuty's core workflow — alert triage, on-call routing, and incident coordination — as among the most automatable enterprise tasks for AI agents.
